content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To      :   File                                                    Date   :Jan. 25, 1977




From  :   FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
           Ted Prim

Subject:  Conversation with Judy Rosner
           on January 25, 1977.

           I explained to her the disclosure obligations imposed by
           the Act relative to investments and interests in real proper-
           ty owned by a trust.  In the instant case, her husband and
           her children own 100% of the income and remainder interest
           of the trust which owns the stocks.  Accordingly, I advised
           her she would have to list all of the stocks of $1,000 or
           more owned by the trust.

           With respect to the interests in real property, her husband
           owns 100% of the life estate and her children own 50% of
           the remainder interest.  Accordingly, she will be deemed
           the owner of the property on which the bank sits and would
           have to disclose it.


           TP:jo

MRS. JUDY B. ROSENER
125 VIA VENEZIA
NEWPORT BEACH, CALIFORNIA 92603

January 26, 1977

Mr. Ted Primm
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Ted,

As per our telephone conversation, here is a listing of the
stocks owned by the trust as of this date:

Codex--OTC
Computer Automation--OTC
Computer Science--NY
Dynatech--OTC
Electronic Engineering of California--American
E.R.C. Corporation--OTC
Inexco--NY
Mercury General--OTC
Progressive Corporation--OTC
Siliconix--OTC
R.R. Industries---American
A.P.L. Corporation--NY
Loews, Inc.--NY
Reliance Electronic 1.60 pfd--NY

The land which should be added to the interests in Real Property
sheet is:

1800 Van Ness Avenue, San Francisco, California
It is valued as more than $100,000.

My husband informs me that he is the beneficiary, and then the
children. I have listed the income from the trust on Schedule
721-D-(Herbert Rosener Trust)

I just realized that I did not list my husband's income under 721-D,
just mine. If that is necessary, it is from the Moxon, Inc.
2222 MICHELSON Drive, Irvine, Calif. 92664. The income is greater
than $10,000. I don't have any more forms, nor do I have the
instructions, so if you want me to do it all over, send me the
appropriate forms and how I should fill them in and I will re-do
it.

Thanks for your interest.

Judy B. Rosener

MRS. JUDY B. ROSENER
125 VIA VENEZIA
NEWPORT BEACH, CALIFORNIA 92660

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California

JAN 12 1976 4:44 PM

January 5, 1977

To whom it may concern:

I have recently been appointed to the State Coastal Commission after
having been a member of the South Coast Regional Commission.

When appointed to the South Coast Commission, I was told to file with
the understanding that our jurisdiction was the coastal zone (1,000
yds. inland from mean high tide) in the Los Angeles and Orange
Counties. I did this, and listed the real property we own. I was
told it was not necessary to list investments unless they were located
in our jurisdiction.

Now I am informed that in filing Form 720 for my new appointment
to the State Commission, that the jurisdiction is the whole state.
Therefore, I have in order to be safe, just listed all of the stocks
which we personally own because I do not know where they do business.
This will be in conflict with the 721 form. Is this a problem?

Also, I need an opinion from your office about how or if to report
the minimal income (less than $2,000/yr. for the last 8 years) which
we receive from a trust created under the will of my deceased father-
in-law. The trust is composed of 15-20 stocks at any given time which
are traded frequently. The paperwork indicating the transactions
is 6" high for any year, and to record all of the transactions would
seem counterproductive to the intent of Proposition 9. Neither I nor
my husband are beneficiaries of the trust. At the death of my husband,
I am bypassed, and the income goes to our children, who upon a certain
age receive the cash value of the trust. How should this be listed?

We also receive income from a trust which consists of a piece of
property at 1800 Van Ness in San Francisco. We do not own the property
(the trust does), but we receive rent from the bank which occupies the
land. When my husband dies, this income goes to our children. In 1990
the real property goes (1/2 interest in the property) to our three
children. Hence, I have no interest other than the income (approximately
$11,000/yr) which my husband now receives. How should it be listed?

I would appreciate any help you can give me so that I have the infor-
mation needed to file correctly. I am assuming that this filing will
be sufficient for me to act as a Commissioner upon taking office on
January 12th, and that should I need to make any changes, after being
advised, I will do so.

Sincerely,

Judy B. Rosener

* She trust is managed by a broker
purposely so that income will NOT be
generated. She trust's valued at
approximately $50,000.

File
Feb. 8, 1977
p. 2

5. I advised her that all investments held from the period
May 5 to December 31, 1976, must be disclosed. However,
acquisition and disposal dates need be listed only for
those pieces of property acquired or disposed of during
that period.

6. I told her to disclose rights offerings in the following
fashion. Assume that the judge has an investment in
PG and E of over $1000. If he accepted a rights offering
during the period, he should list the date the offering
was accepted as the acquisition date and then note on the
statement that he acquired X number of shares on that date.

7. I told her that proceeds from life insurance policies and
savings accounts were not reportable as income.

TP:jo

sections.question: When appointed to the South Coast Commission, I was told to file with the understanding that our jurisdiction was the coastal zone (1,000 yds. inland from mean high tide) in the Los Angeles and Orange Counties. Now I am informed that in filing Form 720 for my new appointment to the State Commission, that the jurisdiction is the whole state. Therefore, I have in order to be safe, just listed all of the stocks which we personally own because I do not know where they do business. This will be in conflict with the 721 form. Is this a problem? Also, I need an opinion from your office about how or if to report the minimal income (less than $2,000/yr. for the last 8 years) which we receive from a trust created under the will of my deceased father-in-law. The trust is composed of 15-20 stocks at any given time which are traded frequently. Neither I nor my husband are beneficiaries of the trust. At the death of my husband, I am bypassed, and the income goes to our children. How should this be listed? We also receive income from a trust which consists of a piece of property at 1800 Van Ness in San Francisco. We do not own the property (the trust does), but we receive rent from the bank which occupies the land. When my husband dies, this income goes to our children. In 1990 the real property goes (1/2 interest in the property) to our three children. Hence, I have no interest other than the income (approximately $11,000/yr) which my husband now receives. How should it be listed?
sections.conclusion: I explained to her the disclosure obligations imposed by the Act relative to investments and interests in real property owned by a trust. In the instant case, her husband and her children own 100% of the income and remainder interest of the trust which owns the stocks. Accordingly, I advised her she would have to list all of the stocks of $1,000 or more owned by the trust. With respect to the interest in real property, her husband owns 100% of the life estate and her children own 50% of the remainder interest. Accordingly, she will be deemed the owner of the property on which the bank sits and would have to disclose it.
sections.question_synthetic: How should newly appointed State Coastal Commission member Judy Rosener disclose investments in trusts and real property interests where she has indirect beneficial interests, and must she disclose all stocks statewide when her prior filing only covered the coastal zone?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC advised that Rosener must disclose all trust-owned stocks valued at $1,000 or more because her family holds 100% of income and remainder interests. For the real property, she must disclose it as an asset because her family's beneficial interests in the trust (her husband's life estate and children's remainder interest) make her the deemed owner.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request letter and FPPC response memoranda. Question and conclusion sections are present but somewhat fragmented across multiple document pages. Extraction confidence lowered due to document quality and formatting issues that made some text illegible.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: When appointed to the South Coast Commission, I was told to file with the understanding that our jurisdiction was the coastal zone (1,000 yds. inland from mean high tide) in the Los Angeles and Orange Counties. Now I am informed that in filing Form 720 for my new appointment to the State Commission, that the jurisdiction is the whole state. Therefore, I have in order to be safe, just listed all of the stocks which we personally own because I do not know where they do business. This will be in conflict with the 721 form. Is this a problem? Also, I need an opinion from your office about how or if to report the minimal income (less than $2,000/yr. for the last 8 years) which we receive from a trust created under the will of my deceased father-in-law. The trust is composed of 15-20 stocks at any given time which are traded frequently. Neither I nor my husband are beneficiaries of the trust. At the death of my husband, I am bypassed, and the income goes to our children. How should this be listed? We also receive income from a trust which consists of a piece of property at 1800 Van Ness in San Francisco. We do not own the property (the trust does), but we receive rent from the bank which occupies the land. When my husband dies, this income goes to our children. In 1990 the real property goes (1/2 interest in the property) to our three children. Hence, I have no interest other than the income (approximately $11,000/yr) which my husband now receives. How should it be listed?

CONCLUSION: I explained to her the disclosure obligations imposed by the Act relative to investments and interests in real property owned by a trust. In the instant case, her husband and her children own 100% of the income and remainder interest of the trust which owns the stocks. Accordingly, I advised her she would have to list all of the stocks of $1,000 or more owned by the trust. With respect to the interest in real property, her husband owns 100% of the life estate and her children own 50% of the remainder interest. Accordingly, she will be deemed the owner of the property on which the bank sits and would have to disclose it.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to Judy Rosener's questions about disclosure obligations for trust-held investments and real property interests upon her appointment to the State Coastal Commission. The FPPC advised on the treatment of stocks in family trusts, real property interests, income reporting, and investment disclosures.
